data.title: RBC sees positive environment for US automobiles ahead of tariff implementation
data.description:
** Brokerage RBC Capital Markets says that March U.S. dealer inventory and pricing data received a boost due to buying before tariffs take effect on April 3

** Says, average transaction price (ATP) "is supportive of a healthy consumer and pent-up post pandemic demand"

** Strong ATPs also support original equipment makers being able to pass through tariffs to consumers, at least for a period - brokerage

** U.S. President Donald Trump's administration on Wednesday confirmed that his 25% global car and truck tariffs will take effect as scheduled on Thursday and that duties on automotive parts imports will be kick in on May 3

** U.S. buyers snapped up more pickup trucks and sport-utility vehicles in the first quarter from a year earlier in the run-up to President Donald Trump's tariffs on auto imports, which could raise prices by thousands of dollars

** As of last close, shares of General Motors and Tesla have lost nearly 10% and 30% YTD respectively while Ford Motor has gained ~4% compared to a 28% drop in the Dow Jones U.S. Automobiles Index (.DJUSAU) in 2025

data.title: Trump's tariffs set to drive up bar bills and cut jobs
data.description:
Key points:
  • Further measures add to drinks industry pain
  • Some shares open flat or even higher as higher tariffs avoided
  • Many drinks have to be made in specific areas, cannot move output to US

From Negroni cocktails to Scotch whisky, costs will rise, sales will fall, jobs will be cut, and drinks brands will disappear from U.S. bar menus as a result of President Donald Trump's reciprocal tariffs, industry bodies said on Thursday.

Trump set out the latest round of U.S. tariffs on Wednesday, upending a globalised trade system and adding to the stress on the world economy, including on the alcohol sector and its biggest players, such as Diageo and Heineken .

In addition to global levies, Trump announced a 25% tariff on beer imports, as well as higher tariffs for EU imports to the U.S., and the alcohol sector is further hit by the inclusion of empty beer cans in existing aluminium tariffs.

Compounding the problem, many categories of drinks have to be made in specific countries or regions so manufacturers cannot dodge tariffs by shifting production to the United States.

Edward Mundy, analyst at Jefferies, noted the worst threats of a 200% tariff on European alcohol and 25% tariffs affecting Mexican tequila and Canadian whisky had not materialised. Spirits stocks opened flat or even positive on Thursday.

But industry bodies said the levies laid out on Wednesday were already enough to inflict major damage.

U.S. sales of French wine and spirits would slide by at least 20%, one group said, while Italian trade association Federvini said it was bracing to relive the "economic trauma" caused by tariffs in Trump's first term, when exports fell by 50%.

"Many labels, which cannot be replaced by local production, will disappear from the tables of U.S. consumers, while a serious production and employment crisis is looming in Italy and Europe," Federvini President Micaela Pallini said in a statement.

Diageo, Heineken and other major spirits and beer producers either declined to comment or did not immediately respond to Reuters' requests.

Chris Swonger, president and CEO of the Distilled Spirits Council of the United States, said the spirits industry has enjoyed zero for zero tariffs largely for decades and needed to be disentangled from the trade problems Trump wanted to solve.

data.title: RxSight Preliminary Q1 Revenue Increases; Lowers 2025 Revenue Guidance
data.description:
RxSight reported preliminary Q1 revenue late Wednesday of $37.9 million, up 28% from a year earlier.

Analysts polled by FactSet expect $39.6 million .

The company also lowered its 2025 revenue guidance range to between $160 million and $175 million, down from its previous projection of $185 million to $197 million.

Analysts surveyed by FactSet expect $187.8 million.

data.title: Turkey Inflation Continues to Ease as Political Storms Cloud Central Bank's PathUpdate
data.description:
By Joshua Kirby

Inflation inched down in Turkey, suggesting the country remains on the path to stabler prices despite the threat from recent political turmoil.

Annual inflation was 38.1% in March, easing from 39.1% in February, Turkey's statistics authority said Thursday. That was a little lower than economists had expected, according to a poll compiled by FactSet.

Continued easing in inflation suggests the recent arrest of a high-profile opposition politician hasn't yet had a major effect on investor confidence or the Turkish lira. Turkish authorities last month imprisoned President Recep Tayyip Erdogan's main rival in the coming presidential election, Istanbul Mayor Ekrem Imamoglu, on what the opposition says are politically motivated charges, triggering mass protests across Turkey and further arrests.

It also led to a sell-off in the lira, but that hasn't yet led to a fresh inflation spike, Capital Economics' William, Jackson said in a note.

"This data release will provide welcome relief for the Turkish central bank ahead of its next meeting," Jackson said. "[It] probably suggests that interest rates will be left unchanged, rather than raised further."

Some experts had warned that markets could be spooked by the prospect of greater interference from Erdogan, who until 2023 had insisted on an unorthodox policy of lower interest rates that he said would work to bring inflation lower. The bank in 2023 reversed course and began to hike its key rate repeatedly, and has seen some success in bringing inflation down from recent peaks. It began to lower rates again at the end of 2024, taking the one-week repo rate to 42.5% last month.

"Investors are worried that these economic policies could be knocked off course by the volatility that's been unleashed," said Hamish Kinnear, an analyst with risk-intelligence agency Verisk Maplecroft.

"The question now is whether Erdogan will allow the central bank to continue its policies or if he will eventually revert to his past tendencies of intervention," Kinnear said.

The bank raised its overnight rate in an off-schedule meeting following Imamoglu's detention, and some economists have speculated it might have to lift its key rate too if the political turmoil threatens investors confidence and further weakens the lira. Tariffs on exports to the U.S. mark a fresh point of uncertainty, though Turkish imports are to be subject only to the 10% baseline, according to the tariff package set out by President Trump on Wednesday.

"I think [the central bank] should hike," said Timothy Ash, an economist focused on Turkey, in a post on X. The bank's policy committee is due to set out its next rate decision on April 17.

Write to Joshua Kirby at joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby

data.title: Gold Retreats But Holds Near Record Highs After Trump's Tariffs
data.description:
By Giulia Petroni

Gold prices pulled back in early trade but remained near record highs as investors seek refuge in the safe-haven asset following U.S. President Trump's latest tariff blitz.

Futures slipped 0.6% to $3,148.10 a troy ounce, likely due to some profit-taking after hitting a fresh peak of $3,196.60 earlier in the trading session.

The Trump administration plans to impose sweeping new duties on trillions of imports, with a baseline levy of 10% and larger reciprocal tariffs on some countries, including China and the European Union. Bullion was exempted from the new tariffs, according to a White House fact sheet.

The precious metal--often viewed as a hedge against economic turmoil--gained more than 19% so far this year. The rally has been fueled by a combination of safe-haven and risk-off buying due to economic and geopolitical uncertainty, as well as robust central bank purchases and inflows into gold-backed exchange traded funds.

Concerns over potential retaliatory measures from U.S. trading partners, which could further escalate global trade tensions, are expected to continue supporting the yellow metal. "Gold can stay well bid this year as geopolitical risks dovetail with economic and policy uncertainty and trade risks to buoy prices," says James Steel, chief precious metals analyst at HSBC.

Write to Giulia Petroni at giulia.petroni@wsj.com

data.title: Gilt Yields in the UK Fall
data.description:
The UK’s 10-year gilt yield dropped to 4.57%, its lowest level in about a month, as President Trump’s latest tariff announcement sparked a broad flight to safety and deepened concerns about its impact on the global economy.

The US is set to impose a 10% tariff on all imports, with significantly higher rates for certain countries.

UK imports will be subject to the 10% tariff.

In response, traders have increased their bets on further rate cuts from the Bank of England.

Markets are now pricing in approximately 62bps of reductions to the BoE's benchmark Bank Rate by December, up from around 54 bps on Wednesday.

The probability of a 25bps rate cut at the BoE’s next monetary policy meeting in May has also risen to about 77%.

data.title: 16% of 1.8 Million German Traders Just Ditched Their Brokers - Here's Why
data.description:
Germany's online investing market has rebounded with a 3% increase in investor numbers, reaching 1.79 million participants after experiencing a decline in 2024. The growth comes as investors return to the market with a renewed focus on long-term financial goals and retirement planning.

Germany's Online Investing Market Rebounds to 1.8 Million Traders

According to Investment Trends' 15th annual Germany Online Investing Report, the market recovery is being fueled by three key segments: first-time investors entering the market, previously dormant investors returning to active trading, and continued engagement from existing investors.Lorenzo Vignati, Associate Research Director at Investment Trends

"This year's online investor participation rebound highlights the resilience of the German investing public," said Lorenzo Vignati, Associate Research Director at Investment Trends. "We're seeing a return of previously inactive investors alongside a strong influx of new market participants—together reshaping the market landscape."

It is worth emphasizing that these data pertain to the entire investment market in Germany. As for the purely CFD market, these values are much smaller, and according to Investment Trends statistics from previous years, fewer than 85,000 investors actively trade such contracts.

You may also like: Why 50,000 Retail Traders are Rushing Back to Hong Kong Markets

Changing Investor Motivations

The latest wave of new and reactivated online investors in Germany is displaying distinctly different motivations compared to previous years. These investors are primarily drawn to platforms that allow small-amount investing and support long-term savings goals. ETFs have emerged as the preferred investment vehicle among this group, while interest in individual shares has waned.

“This shift towards long-term savings goals and low-entry investing reflects a more purposeful mindset among new and returning online investors in Germany,” added Vignati. “They’re not just testing the waters—they’re entering the market with clear goals.”

Word-of-mouth has gained significant traction as a key entry trigger, particularly among newer investors, highlighting the growing influence of peer networks in driving market participation. This social validation appears to be playing an increasingly important role in bringing new participants into the online investing space.

For brokers, the shifting investor mindset presents both challenges and opportunities. The priority must be supporting these goal-oriented investors with accessible tools that build confidence and foster long-term engagement rather than simply focusing on short-term trading capabilities.

Record-Breaking Broker Switching

Perhaps the most striking finding in the report is the unprecedented level of broker switching, which has reached an all-time high of 16%. This significant increase signals a highly fluid and competitive environment in Germany's online investing landscape.

“This elevated churn reflects a more discerning online investor in Germany—one actively seeking platforms that deliver on both value and innovation,” said Vignati. “Brokers must go beyond cost competitiveness to build loyalty, focusing on simplicity, transparency, and solutions that meet rising expectations.”

Brokers now face the challenge of differentiating themselves beyond cost, with simplicity, transparency, and innovative solutions becoming critical factors in retaining clients in this highly competitive environment.

Market Outlook

While investor numbers are rebounding, the report notes that capital gain expectations for domestic equities continue to decline. This suggests that despite returning to the market, German investors maintain a cautious outlook on potential returns.

The research, based on a quantitative online survey of 11,680 German online investors and traders conducted between January and February 2025, provides a comprehensive view of the evolving German investment landscape as it enters a new phase of growth driven by long-term focused participants.

data.title: Ukraine's key sunoil exports down 20% in March y/y, traders union says
data.description:
Ukraine, the world's largest exporter of sunflower oil, reduced sunoil exports to 503,000 metric tons in March from 629,000 tons from the same month a year earlier, Ukrainian traders union UGA said on Thursday.

The union gave no reason for the decrease while analysts have said that a jump in local sunflower seed prices caused by a smaller harvest has forced some sunoil refineries to suspend production and exports.

Refineries have switched to processing soybeans after a record 6.2 million ton harvest in 2024.

UGA said soybean oil exports rose to 57,000 tons in March 2025 against 31,000 tons in March 2024.

The Ukrainian UCAB agricultural business association said earlier this year that Ukraine's sunoil exports could fall to 4.74 million tons in the 2024/25 season from 6.25 million tons in 2023/24 as the sunseed harvest fell to 11 million tons from 14 million.

UCAB said this week Ukraine's overall agricultural exports in March rose 9.8% from the previous month to 5.4 million metric tons.

It said that all product groups showed growth compared to the previous month, but the largest increase was in oilseeds processing products.

data.title: Chinese Shares Fall on Trump's Additional Tariffs
data.description:
Chinese shares fell Thursday after US President Donald Trump imposed new tariffs on goods from China and Hong Kong.

The Shanghai Composite Index, the main gauge of Chinese stocks, slowed 0.24% or by 8.12 points to 3,342.01. The Shenzhen Component Index declined 1.40% or by 147.39 points to 10,365.73.

The White House imposed a 34% reciprocal tariff on China, in addition to the existing 20% levies on the country, bringing the total tariffs on Chinese imports to 54%.

The reciprocal tariffs on China also "apply equally to articles" of both Hong Kong and Macau.

Additionally, the US eliminated the de minimis exemption on Chinese goods, imposing initially a 30% tariff or a $25 fee per postal item on imported goods originating from China and Hong Kong priced at $800 and below and processed through the international postal network.

The duty will be increased to $30 per postal item.

In response, China said it firmly opposes the latest action, with the Ministry of Commerce warning that it will adopt countermeasures to safeguard China's rights and interests.

The duties take effect on April 9.

Meanwhile, China's service sector expanded for the 27th straight month, with the Caixin China General Services Business Activity Index rising to 51.9 points in March from 51.4 in February.

The Caixin China General Composite PMI, which includes both manufacturing and services, increased to 51.8 in March from 51.5 in February.

In corporate news, Luxshare Precision Industry's shares fell 10% following the US tariff announcement. The company, which is reportedly considering a Hong Kong listing this year, assembles iPhones for US tech giant Apple.

Elsewhere, Yunnan Tin's shares closed 4% lower after recording a 2.6% decline in 2024 profit to 1.44 billion yuan.

data.title: Tariffs Raise Inflation Uncertainty, ECB's De Guindos Says
data.description:
By Paul Hannon

The eurozone's inflation rate is heading down toward the European Central Bank's target, but higher U.S. tariffs are a new source of uncertainty, Vice President Luis de Guindos said Thursday.

In a speech in Amsterdam, de Guindos said most recent data suggests the annual rate of inflation will settle at 2% "on a sustained basis." Figures released Tuesday showed the eurozone's inflation rate fell to 2.2% in March from 2.3% in February.

However, President Trump's Wednesday announcement of a new round of tariff increases, including a 20% duty on imports from the European Union, makes it more difficult to predict the future pace of price rises, he said.

"Uncertainty surrounding the inflation outlook remains high, mainly on account of increasing friction in global trade," he said.

De Guindos said a weakening of the euro in response to higher tariffs could push prices of imports higher, although other consequences of the increase in duties would have the opposite effect.

"Lower demand for euro-area exports and lower growth resulting from the impact of higher tariffs or geopolitical tensions could pose a threat to the economy, depress demand and push inflation down," he said.

Write to Paul Hannon at paul.hannon@wsj.com

data.title: German services sector grows modestly in March, PMI shows
data.description:
Germany's service sector maintained modest growth in March, extending its expansion streak to four months, though the pace slowed as new business continued to decline, S&P Global reported on Thursday.

The final HCOB Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) for the services sector fell to 50.9 in March from 51.1 in February, staying slightly above the 50.0 threshold that indicates growth.

"The service sector seems to be losing momentum, though it was not exactly racing ahead before," said Cyrus de la Rubia, Chief Economist at Hamburg Commercial Bank AG.

Firms faced challenges with new business inflows dropping at the fastest rate in six months, driven by heightened uncertainty and slow decision-making among customers.

The decline was partly attributed to reduced foreign orders, exacerbated by tough market conditions, competition and a stronger euro.

Employment continued to rise, with the pace of job creation slightly accelerating from the previous month, the survey showed.

Looking ahead, firms expressed increased optimism, buoyed by expectations of fiscal expansion in Germany and hopes for growth driven by new products, services, and technological change.

"The fact that hiring is still happening and confidence in future business activity has even ticked up suggests we are not looking at a long-term slump," de la Rubia said.

data.title: Italian Private Sector Expands at Softer Pace in March
data.description:
Italy's private sector activity remained in the expansion territory in March for the second month in a row as services output growth offset the continued decline in manufacturing production.

The HCOB Italy Composite PMI Output Index stood at 50.5, down from 51.9 in February, Hamburg Commercial Bank and S&P Global said Thursday.

On the services side, the PMI was 52, below the prior 53 and the consensus estimate of 52.5.

data.title: Ecuhold Completes Acquisition of Additional Stake in Ecu Worldwide Japan
data.description:
Allcargo Logistics said Ecuhold has acquired an additional 10% stake in Ecu Worldwide (Japan) for $220,000, according to a Wednesday filing with the Indian bourses.

Ecuhold now holds a 100% stake in Ecu Worldwide Japan, the filing said.

data.title: Wise targets double-digit pretax profit margin by 2026
data.description:
Wise plc expects to deliver underlying income growth of 15-20% and underlying pretax profit margin at the upper end of its target range in its 2026 financial year, citing strong progress on its "money without borders" vision.

The British money transfer firm, which is hosting a London-based capital markets event on Thursday, said it was targeting underlying pretax profit margin of 13-16% over the medium term and an underlying income compound annual growth rate of 15-20%.

Wise shares were up 5.3% by 0721 GMT.

"Wise customers have moved more than half a trillion across borders and trust us to now hold more than 20 billion pounds of their money," the company said in a statement.

Thursday's presentation will include preliminary figures for its 2025 financial year that will show 21% growth year-on-year in active customers to 15.5 million, a 22% rise in cross-border volumes to 145 billion pounds and a 16% rise in underlying income to 1.36 billion pounds.

Wise said its board had consulted extensively with shareholders on optimal listing arrangements for the company and expects to share the outcome of a review of its listing arrangements with investors in the coming weeks.

data.title: Italian Private Sector Growth Softens in March
data.description:
The HCOB Italy Composite PMI fell to 50.5 in March 2025 from 51.9 in February, signalling modest growth for the second consecutive month.

The services sector drove growth (PMI at 52), while manufacturing continued to contract (PMI at 46.6).

New orders remained stable as gains in services offset declines in manufacturing.

Employment in the private sector saw only a slight increase.

Service providers continued hiring, but manufacturers cut jobs for a second month.

Both sectors had enough capacity to clear backlogs.

Inflationary pressures strengthened, with operating costs rising at the fastest pace in over two years, particularly in services.

As a result, both manufacturers and service providers raised prices, pushing output charge inflation to its highest level in nearly a year.

data.title: Trump's very, very, very simple way of calculating reciprocal tariffs
data.description:
By Steve Goldstein

President Donald Trump's announcement of reciprocal tariffs - with that massive chart behind him - drew puzzled reactions initially.

How did Vietnam - ostensibly a country that the U.S. would want to cultivate to isolate leading adversary China - get hit with one of the highest tariff rates? How come key ally Japan got slapped with a 24% tariff? How did Israel, which just lowered its tariffs on U.S. goods to literally zero, get hit with a 17% tariff?

Why is a country inhabited by penguins tariffed at all?

Sure, there are things like non-tariff barriers - regulations, sales taxes, currency manipulation - but the White House formula for reciprocal tariffs was much simpler.

It's just what is that country's goods exports to the U.S. divided by the country's trade balance. Then, divide that by two. And don't set any number below 10%.

"Put simply, the bigger the nominal trade deficit a country has with the U.S. adjusted for the absolute size of that country's imports, the bigger the tariff," says George Saravelos, head of currency research at Deutsche Bank.

The U.S. Trade Representative has its own description of the calculation but it amounts to exactly the same thing.

Technically, it makes two additional adjustments.

First, it assumes the price elasticity of import demand is 4 - so, a 1% increase in price would lead to a 4% drop in demand.

Then, and this is an important one, it assumes that every 1% increase in tariffs leads to a 0.25% increase in import prices.

Put a different way, using Morgan Stanley's calculation that the effective tariff rate is now 22%, a rise of roughly 20 points from 2024 levels, that should lead to a 5% surge in import prices - according to Trump administration numbers.

Going back to the USTR reciprocal tariff calculation - 4 multiplied by 0.25% is 1, so, the factors offset each other.

The most ironic part of the USTR message comes in the citations. It cites a paper with this title: "Globalization and the gains from variety" - not a message that was articulated on Wednesday.

-Steve Goldstein

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Swiss Inflation Holds Steady as Tariffs Set to Buffet EconomyUpdate
data.description:
By Ed Frankl

Swiss inflation remained unchanged in March, though the impact of U.S. tariffs threaten to roil the country's economy and push the Swiss National Bank to cut rates further.

Annual inflation stayed at 0.3%, the same as February, matching the lowest level in almost four years, Switzerland's federal statistics office said Thursday.

Trends from increased package-holiday and clothing prices contrasted with declining energy and accommodation prices, it said. Core inflation, which excludes more volatile fresh food and energy prices, remained markedly higher, staying at 0.9% on year for the third-straight month.

But new U.S tariffs announced Wednesday by President Trump are expected to significantly hit the Swiss economy, and potentially bring inflation lower as economic activity slows.

The Trump administration imposed a 31% tariff on Swiss exports to the U.S., higher than the 20% placed on the neighboring European Union. The U.S. is Switzerland's largest goods-export market.

Key Swiss exports, including luxury watches, could be strongly impacted by the tariffs. However, the White House left off another major industry for the Swiss economy--pharmaceuticals--from new duties. Swatch shares fell on Thursday morning trade.

The SNB cut its key interest rate in March to 0.25%, citing increased geopolitical uncertainty, as well as risks that inflation would go lower. SNB President Martin Schlegel in recent months hasn't ruled out negative interest rates.

While the Swiss franc has weakened a little since the tariff announcement--which makes imports to Switzerland more expensive--the more important feature for inflation is the negative impact of tariffs on the economy, Capital Economics economist Adrian Prettejohn said in a note.

"This means inflation will be a little lower than otherwise over the medium term," he said, adding he now anticipates the SNB to lower its policy to zero in June, compared with holding at 0.25% as he previously expected.

Swiss President Karin Keller-Sutter said her government took note of the new duties and would decide soon how to proceed.

"The country's long-term economic interests are paramount. Respect to international law and free trade remain core values," she said on X.

Swiss industry group Economiesuisse said Thursday the new tariffs were harmful and unjustified, would make Swiss exports more expensive and would weaken competitiveness.

Write to Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering U.S. politics. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0349 ET - Germany's DAX fell below the 22,000-point mark for the first time in more than a month, hit by President Trump's plan to impose sweeping tariffs. The German blue-chip index is down 1.5% at 22,046.62, having fallen as low as 21,809.40, on track to close at a multiweek low. The DAX last closed below the 22,000-point level on Feb. 10 amid a rally since the start of the year. "A recovery seems contingent on the European Union and the U.S. finding common ground and possibly achieving some diplomatic progress that currently appears unlikely," CMC Markets' Jochen Stanzl says in a market comment. The Trump administration on Wednesday said it would impose duties of 20% on imports from the EU. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0344 ET - U.K. Prime Minister Keir Starmer says the country will take an economic blow from trade tariffs announced by the Trump administration on Wednesday. "Clearly, there will be an economic impact from the decisions the U.S. has taken, both here and globally," he tells business leaders in Downing Street. The U.S. says it will impose a 10% tariff on goods imports from the U.K., albeit lower than the 20% tariffs placed on the neighboring European Union. "Decisions we take in coming days and weeks will be guided only by our national interest, in the interests of our economy, in the interests of businesses...in the interests of putting money in the pockets of working people," he says. "But I want to be crystal clear--we are prepared." (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0334 ET - Adidas shares tumbled in early trade after the U.S. unveiled tariff rates, levying tariffs of 46% on exports from Vietnam, where Adidas has production sites. According to the company website, Vietnam was the German sportswear brand's main sourcing country in 2024, and 92% of its overall volume was produced in Asia. Adidas trades 9.5% lower at 200.20 euros. (sarah.sloat@wsj.com)

0331 ET - Shares in European banks dropped at market open after President Trump's announcement of an across-the-board 10% tariff on all imports and a 20% tariff on EU goods. The Stoxx Europe 600 Banks Index was down more than 3.5%, compared with a 1.7% fall for the wider Stoxx 600 multi-sector index. Banks Societe Generale, Deutsche Bank and Barclays were among the top fallers. Asia-focused London-listed lenders were also down; Standard Chartered's stock shed 7.7%. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0315 ET - Italian prime minister Giorgia Meloni says that officials will do whatever they can to avoid a trade war with the U.S. after President Trump imposed a 20% levy on EU imports on Wednesday. The tariffs are wrong and not in the interest of either party, Meloni says. "We will do everything we can to work on an agreement with the U.S., with the aim of averting a trade war that would inevitably weaken the West in favor of other global players," the premier says. She adds her government will act in Italy's interests and also work with European partners. (edith.hancock@wsj.com)

0313 ET - Concerns about the impact of U.S. tariffs on European insurers are secondary to wider issues, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a research note. "We view them as second-order relative to the wider challenges posed to economies and capital markets, especially for global insurance businesses that can readjust as trade flows and economic momentum evolve," William Hawkins writes. The first order of worry may be the risk to non-life insurers from higher prices in the U.S. and the inflationary consequences. In the long term, a world of higher inflation and yields is good for insurance fundamentals, he notes. Companies in KBW's coverage generally have strong balance sheets to take advantage of opportunities and keep returning attractive payouts to shareholders, he notes. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0301 ET - Luxury companies, watchmakers and midsize industrial companies seem the most exposed among Swiss exporters to direct impacts from the Trump administration's plan to set a 31% tariff on imports from Switzerland, Vontobel analysts say. Swiss pharmaceutical exports are partially exempt and so are some semiconductors, Vontobel says. Meanwhile, large companies with U.S. production could even benefit, according to Vontobel. "Pure domestic players are a relative safer haven, the analysts say. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0256 ET - Irish bank shares are set to remain volatile as the potential implications of U.S. tariffs on European growth and interest rates play out, Citi says in a research note. The U.S.'s 20% tariffs on goods imports from the EU excludes pharmaceuticals, which might provide a short-term relief for Ireland given that two-thirds of its 2023 U.S. exports were linked to the sector, analyst Borja Ramirez Segura writes. "For the time being, it remains unclear whether the baseline 10% tariff will be applied to drugs, and there may be targeted tariffs on the pharma sector in the near future," he says. AIB Group is best placed among domestic peers in case of weaker economic growth given its higher capital and overlay provisions, the analyst adds. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0244 ET - President Trump's retaliatory tariffs were much higher than expected, and this has investors fearing a significant weakening of global growth, Jyske Markets says. "Investors are fleeing stocks, and especially cyclical sectors, and are seeking protection in bonds and other safe havens such as the yen." Investors will today be watching for reactions from the affected countries in the form of retaliation as well as expansionary fiscal policy or signals of interest rate cuts, it says. Jyske Markets says a quick calculation suggests the tariffs could send the U.S. into recession if they are maintained for a longer period. At the same time, core inflation will rise significantly, and this combination will put the Fed under serious pressure, it says. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0241 ET - The U.S. tariff rate of 31% on Swiss exports is excessively high and represents a serious burden to business, Swiss business lobby Economiesuisse says. "The new tariffs make Swiss exports more expensive, weaken the competitiveness of companies and impact the investment climate," the association says. "This is happening at a time when export prospects in other sales markets are already gloomy." The association argues that Switzerland's import tariffs are already lower than those of the U.S. At around 53 billion Swiss francs ($60 billion), the U.S. is Switzerland's most important export market, according to Economiesuisse. (sarah.sloat@wsj.com)

0238 ET - European stocks are expected to open sharply lower after President Trump announced sweeping tariffs that were greater than market commentators had expected. IG calls the Stoxx Europe 600 index down 1.9%; Germany's DAX index down 1.7%; France's CAC 40 down 1.5%; and the U.K.'s FTSE 100 down 1.2%. The U.S. will impose 10% across-the-board tariffs on all imports and even higher rates for some nations, including 20% for Europe. "Markets have seen a strong risk-off reaction to the tariff announcement," Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid says in a note. "Trump's comments did leave the door open for potential negotiations to lower tariffs but his executive order also left room for further escalation," he says. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0217 ET - Trade association UK Steel says it remains deeply concerned by the imposition of tariffs by U.S. President Trump. "The U.K. government must continue its efforts to strike a deal with the U.S., but we recognize that this requires willingness from both sides," the trade body says. The industry can't wait any longer for government support to address a deepening crisis in the sector, the body says. Steel from the U.K. to the U.S. will continue to face a 25% tariff as introduced in March, it says. Trump announced a slew of additional tariffs Wednesday, adding a 10% import tax on all other goods imported to the U.S. from the country. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Markets Fell on Worse-Than-Expected U.S. Tariffs
data.description:
By Dow Jones Newswires Staff

U.S. stock futures and global equities markets tumbled along with the dollar and Treasury yields following the Trump administration's sweeping tariffs, which were harsher than expected.

  • S&P 500 stock futures were last down around 3% and Dow Jones Industrial Average futures were off 2.2% premarket.
  • Stock markets across Asia, the target of some of President Trump's highest tariffs, fell. The Nikkei Stock Average dropped 2.8% with chip makers and financials leading the falls. Chinese shares ended lower, though perhaps not as low as some had expected, weighed by home-appliances, consumer-products and hardware stocks. South Korea's benchmark Kospi fell 0.8% to close at 2486.70, paring some of its early losses.
  • At the open in Europe, the Stoxx 600 index fell 1.3% with bigger falls in sub-indexes for tech, banks and industrial goods. Sporting goods, luxury and autos were all hit. On the upside, Novartis shares rose 1.5% and GSK shares were up 1.15% after medicines got an exemption from the tariffs.
  • At the open, the U.K.'s FTSE 100 lost 1.3% after the country got the lowest-tier 10% tariff. France's CAC 40 fell 2.2% and Germany's DAX fell 2.4%.
  • The dollar fell to a near six-month low. The tariff outcome "was worse than expected and it raises the risk for a U.S. recession," Danske Bank analyst Stefan Mellin said in a note. The DXY dollar index fell to a low of 102.728 against a basket of major currencies.
  • The Canadian dollar rose to a five-week high against the U.S. dollar and the Mexican peso hit a one-week high after the two countries were exempt from further tariffs. Trump had previously announced a 25% levy on a large share of Canada and Mexico imports.
  • U.S. Treasurys rallied. The 10-year Treasury yield dropped 15 basis points to 4.047%.
  • In commodities, gold futures hit a new record high earlier in the session of $3,196.60 a troy ounce. Oil headed the other way as global demand fears mounted; in early European trade, Brent crude dropped 3.2% to $72.53 a barrel, while West Texas Intermediate was down 3.4% at $69.24 a barrel.
  • Notably, Tesla shares fell more than 8% in after-hours trading, to $260.10.

Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: Germany's DAX Drops on Tariff FearsMarket Talk
data.description:
0749 GMT - Germany's DAX fell below the 22,000-point mark for the first time in more than a month, hit by President Trump's plan to impose sweeping tariffs. The German blue-chip index is down 1.5% at 22,046.62, having fallen as low as 21,809.40, on track to close at a multiweek low. The DAX last closed below the 22,000-point level on Feb. 10 amid a rally since the start of the year. "A recovery seems contingent on the European Union and the U.S. finding common ground and possibly achieving some diplomatic progress that currently appears unlikely," CMC Markets' Jochen Stanzl says in a market comment. The Trump administration on Wednesday said it would impose duties of 20% on imports from the EU. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0734 GMT - Adidas shares tumbled in early trade after the U.S. unveiled tariff rates, levying tariffs of 46% on exports from Vietnam, where Adidas has production sites. According to the company website, Vietnam was the German sportswear brand's main sourcing country in 2024, and 92% of its overall volume was produced in Asia. Adidas trades 9.5% lower at 200.20 euros. (sarah.sloat@wsj.com)

0718 GMT - U.S. tariffs open up a 16% downside to 2025-2026 EBIT across the automakers, led by Porsche, Stellantis and Mercedes-Benz, Citi analysts write. Between Europe, Canada, and Mexico, European manufacturers export around 1.3 million cars into the U.S. with a combined revenue stream of around 70 billion euros, it says. A 25% tariff applied to all imports poses a significant gross cost increase. However, with offsets from much lower transfer prices on which tariffs are charged, likely higher prices for U.S. sales, a change in mix towards higher priced models, and dealer and other cost reductions, Citi thinks the net EBIT impact could potentially be in the region of 10 billion euros over 2025 and 2026. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0701 GMT - Luxury companies, watchmakers and midsize industrial companies seem the most exposed among Swiss exporters to direct impacts from the Trump administration's plan to set a 31% tariff on imports from Switzerland, Vontobel analysts say. Swiss pharmaceutical exports are partially exempt and so are some semiconductors, Vontobel says. Meanwhile, large companies with U.S. production could even benefit, according to Vontobel. "Pure domestic players are a relative safer haven, the analysts say. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0656 GMT - Irish bank shares are set to remain volatile as the potential implications of U.S. tariffs on European growth and interest rates play out, Citi says in a research note. The U.S.'s 20% tariffs on goods imports from the EU excludes pharmaceuticals, which might provide a short-term relief for Ireland given that two-thirds of its 2023 U.S. exports were linked to the sector, analyst Borja Ramirez Segura writes. "For the time being, it remains unclear whether the baseline 10% tariff will be applied to drugs, and there may be targeted tariffs on the pharma sector in the near future," he says. AIB Group is best placed among domestic peers in case of weaker economic growth given its higher capital and overlay provisions, the analyst adds. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0644 GMT - President Trump's retaliatory tariffs were much higher than expected, and this has investors fearing a significant weakening of global growth, Jyske Markets says. "Investors are fleeing stocks, and especially cyclical sectors, and are seeking protection in bonds and other safe havens such as the yen." Investors will today be watching for reactions from the affected countries in the form of retaliation as well as expansionary fiscal policy or signals of interest rate cuts, it says. Jyske Markets says a quick calculation suggests the tariffs could send the U.S. into recession if they are maintained for a longer period. At the same time, core inflation will rise significantly, and this combination will put the Fed under serious pressure, it says. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0622 GMT - Nordic markets are seen opening sharply lower with IG calling the OMXS30 down 2% at around 2429. President Trump's proposed tariffs are higher than SEB assumed and this will have a negative impact on growth, analysts at the bank say in a note. "It is too early to make a full assessment, but we still do not see at this point that it will trigger a U.S. recession." Stock futures signal falling stock markets as U.S. and European markets open, and Asian stock markets have fallen overnight. Oil prices have fallen more than 3% while gold has hit new highs, SEB says. "Today's agenda will of course be dominated by the aftermath of the USA's announced tariffs, where for example the EU will make a statement at 1700 CET." OMXS30 closed at 2478.69, OMXN40 at 2374.99 and OBX at 1426.35. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0558 GMT - PDD Holdings' Temu, which has a significant exposure to the U.S., is likely to be the most affected by higher U.S. tariffs among Chinese tech names, Citi analysts say in a research note. The analysts note Temu has taken some measures ahead of time to address challenges, such as shifting its U.S. model to a semi-managed model, where merchants have some flexibility to operate and manage their inventories over the past few months. Many sellers have already front-loaded some inventory for storage at U.S. warehouses. However, the steeper-than-expected tariffs and removal of the de minimis exemption will still cause significant uncertainty for the Temu business over time, Citi says. PDD's ADRs last closed at $119.07. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0547 GMT - The increased U.S. tariffs may affect some Asian sportswear brands if they become permanent, Morningstar analyst Ivan Su writes in a note. The additional tariffs pose challenges for selected sportswear companies including Shenzhou International, Amer Sports and Asics, Su adds. Shenzhou International is a key supplier for major sportswear brands and 17% of its revenue comes from the U.S. with most of its products manufactured in Vietnam, he says. If Shenzhou International shares the higher tariff costs equally with its customers, that would have a 20% earnings impact in the worst-case scenario, he adds. Meanwhile, since Amer and Asics' U.S. margins are below group averages, the earnings impact will be capped at 15% for Amer and 10% for Asics, Morningstar estimates.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0543 GMT - The U.K. Government needs to double efforts and work urgently to negotiate a mutually beneficial economic agreement with the U.S. to reverse the tariffs charged on exported goods, chief executive of manufacturing trade body Make UK, Stephen Phipson, says. The tariffs will destroy decades of integrated supply chains connecting the U.K. with U.S. through other trading partners who are also impacted by the tariffs, he says. "This isn't the time for a trade war, we need to find a way to mitigate against these crippling tariffs at speed," Phipson says. (ian.walker@wsj.com.)

0539 GMT - U.S. tariffs represent a massive burden for auto companies and supply chains and mark a fundamental break in trade policy, says Hildegard Mueller, president of German automotive industry association VDA. Germany exported about 450,000 cars to the U.S. in 2024, according to VDA. The German auto industry also has more than 2,000 sites in the U.S., employing around 138,000 people. By snubbing the global trade order, the U.S. turns away from the basis for value creation and corresponding growth in many regions, Mueller says. "This is not America first, this is America alone," Mueller says. She calls on the EU to present a united front, while signaling its willingness to negotiate. (sarah.sloat@wsj.com)

0523 GMT - Trade organization spiritsEurope says it supports any efforts made by the European Union to reach a negotiated solution with the U.S. as the spirits sector braces for the tariffs announced by President Trump. The industry is highly integrated across the Atlantic, spiritsEurope says, making the sector particularly vulnerable to tariffs. The association, which represents the sector in the EU, says it is prepared to contribute with a proposal to improve the fortunes of the industry on both sides of the Atlantic."We want to ensure a return to an environment that supports continued growth for both EU and U.S. producers," the association adds. (michael.susin@wsj.com)

data.title: Global drinks maker Suntory aims to sell local to avoid tariffs, president says
data.description:
Key points:
  • Suntory to shift marketing strategy amid potential trade war
  • U.S. tariffs impact Japanese goods, including Suntory's whiskies
  • Suntory focuses on organic growth, no acquisitions planned

Japanese drinks maker Suntory is looking to shift where it markets its spirits across the globe as it girds for a potential trade war, its new president said on Thursday.

Nobuhiro Torii, great-grandson of the company's founder, takes the reins at a raucous time, with new U.S. tariffs poised to affect everything from Toyota's sedans to Suntory's aged whiskies.

The company's soft drinks are mainly produced in their destination markets, but spirits such as tequila must be made in specific locations and are more vulnerable to excise duties, Torii said. That means making bourbon for the American market in the United States, and Japanese whisky for domestically for consumption at home. (SUNTH.UL)

"We will produce and try to sell locally," said Torii, who took over as president at the closely held consumer conglomerate on March 25. "That's the key strategy with the tariffs."

U.S. President Donald Trump revealed on Wednesday a broad set of new tariffs on trading partners, including a larger-than-anticipated 24% levy on Japanese goods. Trade Minister Yoji Muto said on Thursday Japan is leaving all options open to respond to Trump's new tariffs.

The trade rift presents a dilemma for Japanese companies like Suntory, which has spent decades expanding its global footprint as a hedge against an ageing, shrinking consumer market at home.

Suntory spent $16 billion in 2014 to buy U.S. spirits maker Beam, but the group also makes wine and Orangina-branded soda in Europe, as well as Pepsi drinks and canned coffee in Southeast Asia.

As for disruptions from tariffs, Suntory is somewhat protected by the defensive nature of the drinks sector, Torii said.

"People become thirsty when it's hot, and people want to drink beer for relaxing," he said. "Of course, business and the economy itself is possibly becoming more volatile, and there might be a trade war, but still we have to manage that."

As Trump has ramped up tariff rhetoric in recent months, Suntory shipped extra supplies of tequila from its Mexican factories into the U.S. to get in before the excise increases. The company is also considering shifting sales of Scotch whiskies to Europe from the U.S.

Suntory founder Shinjiro Torii established Japan's first whisky distillery in the western city of Yamazaki in 1923. The company name is a combination of "sun" and his surname.

Nobuhiro Torii, 59, earned degrees from Keio University in Tokyo and Brandeis University in Massachusetts, and worked at the Industrial Bank of Japan, now part of the Mizuho banking group, before joining Suntory.

As president, he succeeds Takeshi Niinami, who in 2014 became the first person outside of the founding family to run the group. Niinami, one of Japan's most influential executives, is staying on as chairman and CEO.

Niinami last year said Suntory could be in the market for an overseas purchase on the order of $10 billion, and often spoke of business growth in India and China.

By contrast, Torii said he's more focused on investing in Suntory's existing brands, and has no acquisition candidates in mind.

"It's very easy to say that China and India are attractive, but I still don't know how to make money over there," he said. "Organic growth is our priority at this moment."

data.title: French services sector contracts again in March, PMI shows
data.description:
France's dominant services sector contracted for the seventh consecutive month in March, marking its weakest quarterly performance since the fourth quarter of 2023, S&P Global reported on Thursday.

The HCOB France services PMI rose to 47.9 in March from 45.3 in February, indicating a slowdown in the contraction rate. Readings above 50.0 point to growth.

March's flash services PMI stood at of 46.6 points.

Demand continued to deteriorate, with new business declining for the seventh month in a row. The volume of work in progress fell, prompting firms to reduce staff levels, although the rate of job cuts eased compared to February.

"The first quarter of 2025 ends on a disappointing note for the French services sector," said Dr. Tariq Kamal Chaudhry, economist at Hamburg Commercial Bank AG.

"Despite a slight slowdown in contraction compared to February, economic uncertainty and deteriorating demand weighed heavily on activity levels."

The composite PMI, which includes both manufacturing and services, rose to 48.0 in March from 45.1 in February, indicating a moderate contraction in private sector activity. The final figure was also up from the initial 'flash' reading of 47.0.

Inflationary pressures eased, with input costs showing the smallest increase since October last year, driven mainly by wage costs.

Looking ahead the outlook remains bleak, with both domestic and foreign demand seen continuing to decline. The 12-month activity outlook index held above 50.0 but was well below its long-term average, reflecting weak optimism among service providers.

Some firms expressed hope for lower interest rates and increased client portfolios, but many were concerned about growing uncertainty.

data.title: China's Domestic, Services, Growth Stocks Likely to Be Resilient Amid U.S. TariffsMarket Talk
data.description: China's domestic, services and growth stocks are expected to be more resilient following the larger-than-expected U.S. tariffs, according to Citi analysts in a research note. The tariff increase by on Chinese goods appears to exceed Citi's previous estimates, the analyst note. Amid ongoing uncertainties, Citi favors domestic-focused stocks over export-oriented plays, services over goods sectors given China's economic transformation, and growth over value stocks, since U.S. 10-year Treasury yields could remain above 4%. Citi is overweight on internet, technology and transportation sectors, along with selective consumer stocks. Its top investment picks include Tencent, ASMPT, Trip.com, and others. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

data.title: Investors add to Bank of England rate cut bets as Trump hikes tariffs
data.description:
Investors added to bets on Bank of England interest rate cuts and government bond yields fell sharply as global markets braced for a hit to trade and growth from U.S. President Donald Trump's import tariffs on dozens of countries including Britain.

Interest rate futures on Thursday pointed to about 62 basis points of reductions to the BoE's benchmark Bank Rate by December, compared with around 54 bps on Wednesday which represented a full pricing of two quarter-point rate cuts.

The chance of a quarter-point rate cut at the BoE's next scheduled meeting on monetary policy in May was seen at about 77%, also up from Wednesday.

Two-year gilt yields fell to 4.067% at 0715 GMT, the lowest since October 23 and down about 10 basis points on the day. The 10-year yields touched their lowest since March 4 at 4.533%.

On Wednesday, Trump announced tariffs of 10% on UK exports to the United States, 20% on goods from the European Union and higher duties on other countries including 54% on China.

Britain's government said it was looking to secure a better trading deal with the US.

"The one silver lining is that the retaliatory tariff rate applied to the UK is the lowest among affected countries," Barret Kupelian, chief economist at PwC, said.

"Our European trading partners will face steeper tariffs, though some of that economic pain will inevitably spill over to UK firms through supply chains and shared markets."

data.title: Luxin Venture Capital Group Acquiring 3.8% Stake in Baogang International Port
data.description:
Luxin Venture Capital Group subsidiary Shandong High-Tech Investment will invest 80 million yuan to acquire 3.82% equity in Baogang International Port.

The stake will be acquired from Shanghai Xiangda Equity Investment Fund Management at 8.2 yuan per share, according to a Wednesday filing with the Shanghai bourse.

The transaction is expected to enhance long-term investment returns.
